title: Calendering handmade sheets
subtitle: Smoothing and compressing freshly dried handmade paper to improve its surface for writing and printing
excerpt: Handmade paper fresh from the drying board has a matte, slightly textured surface ideal for watercolour but too absorbent for ink calligraphy. Calendering compresses the fibres between smooth boards to close the surface and reduce absorbency without sizing.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), chapter on finishing and calendering. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/oldpapermak00hunt. Hunter describes the calendering cylinder used in traditional paper mills and notes the equivalent pressure achievable at home scale by nipping between boards in a book press.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of compressing paper between smooth rollers or boards to flatten the surface fibres, reduce surface roughness, and improve the paper's writing or printing quality.
The treatment of paper with a starch, gelatine, or alum solution to reduce the paper's absorbency and make it suitable for ink work. Calendering compresses rather than coats.

content.text: A freshly pulled sheet of handmade paper contains air pockets between the loose fibre network. When the sheet dries without pressure, those pockets remain open and the surface stays rough and absorbent.

closes them by pressing the dried sheet between smooth boards under weight. The fibres flatten together; the surface hardens slightly. The result is paper that takes ink more cleanly without the need for surface

A book press is the home papermaker's equivalent of a calendering cylinder. You also need two smooth, hard boards (plywood faced with smooth melamine or two pieces of glass work well) larger than the sheets being calendered, and sheets of dry blotting paper.

Calender only fully dried sheets. Paper that still contains moisture will cockle and wrinkle under pressure rather than compressing. The sheet should feel dry to the back of the hand and show no cool or damp spots. If you are unsure, allow a further 24 hours of air drying.
Place one smooth board in the base of the book press. Lay a sheet of dry blotting paper on the board.
Lay the handmade sheet on the blotting paper, face up. If calendering multiple sheets, separate each with a blotting paper interleaf.
Place the second smooth board on top. Apply the press. The press should be closed firmly but not to maximum pressure: a moderate compression is the aim, not the full force of the press.
Leave for 30 minutes. Release the press, turn the sheets over, and re-press for a further 30 minutes. Turning ensures both faces receive equal compression.
Remove the sheets and allow them to air fully. The calendered surface should feel noticeably smoother than the uncalendered reverse face if you run a fingertip lightly across both.

Calendered unsized paper is still more absorbent than a commercially sized sheet. It suits journalling with water-based ink, calligraphy practice, and watercolour work where some tooth is wanted. For fine calligraphy with iron-gall ink, follow calendering with alum-gelatine sizing to close the surface fully. Calendering can be done before or after sizing. Done before, it produces a slightly more compact finish.
